title: practical english usage - Part 96

dictionary will often show which  form is correct or most natural.

common expressions will be learnt by experience; in case of doubt a good which  structure is used to express a particular compound idea. The  most in entries 124-127, but, unfortunately, there is no easy way to be quite sure This is a very complicated area of English grammar. General guidelines are given grammar *116   spelling of plurals logo      logos            zoo      zoos kilo      kilos            soprano   sopranos euro      euros            solo      solos concerto   concertos         radio    radios commando  commandos        piano    pianos avocado  avocados         photo     photos Singular       Plural                                    Singular       Plural Most nouns ending in -o have plurals in -s. Examples: 3 plural     of  nouns     ending     in  -o Nouns ending in a single -z have plurals in -zzes: quiz/quizzes, fez/fezzes.

buzz             buzzes box                                             boxes bus                                             buses crash                                          crashes church                                        churches ... ch/sh/s/x/z      ... ches/shes/ses/xes/zes Singular                             Plural monarch/monarchs).

Exceptions: words ending in ch pronounced /k/  (e.g. stomach/stomachs, If the singular ends in -sh, -ch, -s, -x or -z, the plural is made by adding -es.

2 plural     of  nouns     ending     in  -sh,   -ch,   -s,  -x  or  -z not Do you know the Kennedys? (     ... the Kennedies?) I hate Februarys.

Proper names ending in consonant + y usually have plurals in -ys.

by adding -s (days, boys, guys, donkeys).

If the singular ends in vowel + y (e.g. day, boy, guy, donkey), the plural is made party           parties ferry                                           ferries lady                                            ladies baby                                           babies ... consonant + y      ... consonant + ies Singular                              Plural normally made by changing y to i and adding -es.

ɪf the singular ends in consonant + y (for example -by, -dy, -ry, -ty), the plural is 1 plural     of  nouns     ending     in  consonant        + y some special cases.

The  plural of most nouns is made by just adding -s to the singular. But there are 116  spelling      of  plurals grammar «117      irregular and special plurals a singular noun people (plural peoples) meaning 'nation'.

Persons is sometimes used as a plural of person in official language. There is also pounds and one pence, please).

money. Some British people now use pence as a singular (e.g. That'll be three one-cent coins in the USA); pence is used to talk about prices and sums of The  regular plural pennies can be used to talk about separate penny coins (and mouse     mice man      men             woman    women louse     lice             tooth     teeth goose     geese            person    people foot     feet            penny    pence child     children          ox       oxen Singular       Plural                                    Singular       Plural 2 other     irregular       plurals Other words ending in -f(e) have regular plurals, for example roofs, beliefs, safes.
